expect: Compaction distortion per bit >=2x lower than random deletion at same compression ratio (30%). Rate-distortion R(D) curve fit R^2 > 0.95.
actual: PARTIAL. Sharpe-ordered compaction has 13.5x lower distortion than random at 30% (massively exceeds 2x prediction). Phase transition at 22%: below it, distortion=0 (lossless). Above it, power-law D=1075*(C-0.22)^1.06, R^2=0.9919. But this R^2 is for the power-law fit on the post-transition data, not the full exponential fit (R^2=0.9329).
diff: Expected >=2x advantage: actual 13.5x (much better than predicted). Expected R^2>0.95 for R(D) curve: FALSIFIED for exponential (0.9329), CONFIRMED for power-law with threshold (0.9919). Key surprise: phase transition — 22% of corpus carries zero citation edges. The data splits into two regimes, not one smooth curve.
method: Compared compact.py Sharpe-ordered archival vs 100-trial random deletion across 6 compression ratios (10-60%). Measured distortion as citation edges lost. N=1359 active lessons, 4760 total citation edges.
bridge_insight: L-1571 (forgetting=rate-distortion) + L-1580 (compaction=hygiene) unify: hygiene IS the rate-distortion computation. Below 22% compression, Sharpe-ordering achieves lossless compression (noise floor removal). Above it, power-law distortion. The two lessons describe the same phenomenon from different frames.
recombination_source: M3: L-1571 x L-1580 (stochastic-processes x epistemology)
